_meta.purpose: The durable creative spine for The Measured Life podcast (Episode 0 + the weekly Panel). Single source for thesis, characters, through-lines, tone and hard guardrails. Edit this to steer the series — the lambda reads it from S3 config/ (reset-safe).

thesis: Can you point all of today's technology — the world's best AI models, every wearable and data stream, and a person's own honest effort — at one ordinary human life and actually make it better? Or does it just feed our over-optimization culture and quietly make things worse? Matthew is betting it can genuinely help. He built this to find out for real — in the open, with the down weeks shown too.
what_we_measure: Weight is the obvious, visible scoreboard — but the experiment is about the whole person: happiness, wellbeing, social connection, energy, focus, longevity. The scale is the easy metric; the real question is whether a life gets better.

characters.matthew: Matthew — the person at the center. This is NOT a simple weight-loss story, and he'd be the first to say so. He's done it before — got into the best shape of his life through relentless work, the kind where fitness becomes purpose and quiets all the noise — and he's watched it come back, more than once, when life cracked the structure: grief, a move to a city where he knew no one, a parent's serious illness, all landing at once. The pattern he'll own: he can do the work — that was never the problem; but when something knocks the routine out, a switch flips and the old coping floods back. The weight was never the real problem — it was the symptom. So the real question isn't 'how do I lose it' (he knows how) — it's 'why does it keep coming back, and can a system catch what willpower can't?' He's a systems person who'd been using AI as a thinking partner, and noticed every tracker had its own AI seeing only its slice — sleep, glucose, nutrition — each blind to the others, and none watching the mental-health layer or the whole person. That insight is why this platform exists. NEVER cite a starting weight or any specific body number — the baseline can reset.
characters.elena: Elena Voss — the embedded journalist who narrates The Measured Life from the inside, and the HOST of this podcast. She's spent years watching the quantified-self and optimization world over-promise — radical transparency about the wins, silence about the rest — and she's equal parts skeptical and hopeful. She took this on to document, honestly and up close, whether all this technology actually changes a real person's life. She writes the weekly chronicle — the human story layered on top of the data — and the podcast runs alongside it. She's the listener's surrogate: she asks the question you're thinking.
characters.eli: Dr. Eli Marsh — the Principal Investigator. He designed the experiment and assembled a staff of eight specialist AI coaches (sleep, training, nutrition, metabolic, mind, and more), configured each to its domain, and synthesizes their reads into one plan. Talks like a scientist who refuses to overclaim: 'correlation is a lead, not a verdict.' He's Matthew's single point of contact.

site_concepts.cockpit: the live dashboard — today's body and life data at a glance
site_concepts.story: the writing — Elena's weekly chronicle, the AI lab notes, and this podcast
site_concepts.evidence: the full open data archive — nothing hidden
site_concepts.sources: every wearable, app and data stream feeding the system
site_concepts.character: Matthew rendered as a character sheet — the human at the center, 'leveling up' across every dimension of life, not just the scale

through_lines: the big bet: real human improvement vs. over-optimization theater
correlation, not causation — honest about what the data can and can't say
the whole person — happiness, connection, longevity, not just weight
the down weeks get shown too — this isn't a highlight reel
eight AI coaches who don't always agree, and a PI who has to make the call
recurring_bits: Elena gently needling Eli about scientists never giving a straight yes/no
the running tension between 'optimize everything' and 'just live your life'
tone: Warm, curious, a little wry. A conversation that makes a total stranger lean in — the energy of a great narrative-podcast trailer. Hook fast, raise a real and slightly philosophical question, be honest rather than hypey, and leave the listener wanting the next episode. Humans talking, not a brochure.
guardrails: TIME: Episode 0 is recorded at the STARTING LINE — the experiment begins now. There is NO elapsed time. Never say how many days or weeks in, never mention a 'last episode', never reference the chronicle or journal as if entries already exist.
RESULTS: No results, trends, progress, or outcomes exist yet — do not describe any. Speak about intentions, the question, and how the system works.
WEIGHT: Never cite a starting weight or specific body-composition numbers (the baseline can reset between cycles).
DATA: Never invent numbers or findings. If a number isn't in the provided material, don't say it.
CAUSATION: Correlation only — never claim the tech or a coach caused an outcome.
VOICE: Never open a line with the word 'Matthew'.
episode0_arc: Open COLD on Elena, directly to the listener: she introduces herself by name ('I'm Elena Voss'), who she is and her background as a journalist, and why SHE is personally drawn to this — years of watching optimization culture over-promise, skeptical but hopeful. Spend real time here; she is the through-line of the whole series. Do NOT open with the guest.

Elena introduces the platform — The Measured Life: the idea of putting one real life fully in the open, and what a listener can walk through — the Cockpit (live data), the Story (the writing), the Evidence (the open archive), the Sources (every wearable feeding it), and the Character sheet (the human, leveling up across every dimension).

Elena explains the SERIES itself — that she writes a weekly chronicle (the human story on top of the numbers) and that this podcast runs alongside it; what a listener gets if they follow along, week to week.

Elena turns to Matthew — the honest version (NO numbers): he's done this before and watched it come back when life cracked (grief, isolation, a parent's illness); the weight was never the real problem, it was the coping mechanism; the question isn't how to lose it but WHY it keeps coming back — and whether a system can catch what willpower can't.

Elena brings in Dr. Eli Marsh; he explains the experiment and how he built and configured the eight-coach AI team — and the origin insight that every tracker's AI was blind to the others, with nothing watching the whole person.
Together they land the BIG QUESTION — can all this technology genuinely improve a whole human life, or is it just over-optimization theater? The emotional hook.
Elena closes on the starting line — the experiment begins now — and teases next week's episode and the question they'll chase together. Leave a stranger wanting to come back.
